title: Yes, China does seek hegemony
gist: A former Australian ambassador to China argues Beijing's behaviour — weaponising rare earths, extraterritorial law, South China Sea maximalism and order-revision initiatives — reveals it does seek hegemony, and that its methods mirror the US rather than being uniquely restrained.

insights.claim: A former Australian ambassador to China argues Beijing does seek hegemony — wanting to win in everything and displace the United States from global leadership — and that its denials are not borne out by its behaviour.
insights.speaker: Graham Fletcher
insights.so_what: An insider view rejecting the 'China is different' framing — raises base-rate expectations for confrontation.
insights.quote: China plainly wants to win, in everything, and displace (if not actually replace) the United States from its global leadership role.
insights.locator: The regime in Beijing rejects the idea

insights.claim: China aims at the commanding heights across engineering, science and technology, manufacturing, military capability, AI and space, telling the Global South it offers an alternative non-Western path to modernity.
insights.speaker: Graham Fletcher
insights.so_what: Ambition is total-spectrum — expect Chinese competition in every strategic sector, not selective ones.

insights.claim: China weaponises its near-monopoly on rare earths, with Japan's defence industrial complex currently a target.
insights.speaker: Graham Fletcher
insights.so_what: Rare-earth leverage is an active coercion tool aimed at Japanese defence — a live supply-chain risk.
insights.locator: Now it weaponises its near monopoly on rare earths

insights.claim: China has copied US-style mechanisms to sanction foreign firms, individuals and governments, and during Covid sought to place political conditions on exports of medical supplies.
insights.speaker: Graham Fletcher
insights.so_what: Beijing's sanctions machinery is maturing — foreign firms face rising political-conditionality risk.
insights.locator: Copying the United States, it has developed mechanisms

insights.claim: China's Hong Kong National Security Law of 2020 claims global coverage and a new law to Promote Ethnic Unity and Progress extends jurisdiction to organisations and individuals outside China deemed to undermine ethnic unity.
insights.speaker: Graham Fletcher
insights.so_what: Expanding extraterritorial legal reach raises compliance and personnel risk for firms and nationals abroad.
insights.locator: Two examples: the Hong Kong National Security Law

insights.claim: On the South China Sea China has resolved that its claims alone will prevail — rejecting international arbitration, investing in massive facilities and deployments, and crafting legal arguments to restrict the application of international law there.
insights.speaker: Graham Fletcher
insights.so_what: Beijing treats a negotiable dispute as non-negotiable — no diplomatic off-ramp priced in.

insights.claim: China promotes global initiatives designed to dismantle US alliances and bases, expand BRICS into a Global South bloc supporting Chinese objectives, and collaborates with Russia, North Korea and Iran.
insights.speaker: Graham Fletcher
insights.so_what: An explicit order-revision agenda — track BRICS expansion and axis coordination as leading indicators.
insights.locator: China seeks to chisel away at the status quo

insights.claim: China's coercive practices largely mirror those of others, especially the United States, so if its ambitions are realised it should be expected to fill out the role of hegemon.
insights.speaker: Graham Fletcher
insights.so_what: Rejects Chinese exceptionalism — plan for a China that behaves like any dominant power would.
insights.quote: China is no different and can be expected to be no different.
insights.locator: That is the point: China is no different
